records.ety: [L. collecrus, p. p. of collerige to bind together; col- + legere to gather: cf. OF. collecter. See Legend, and cf. Coil, v. t., Cull, v. t.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Collected; p. pr. & vb. n. Collecting.]

defs: To gather into one body or place; to assemble or bring together; to obtain by gathering.
quotes: A band of men Collected choicely from each country.
'Tis memory alone that enriches the mind, by preserving what our labor and industry daily collect.

defs: To demand and obtain payment of, as an account, or other indebtedness; as, to collect taxes.
To infer from observed facts; to conclude from premises.
quotes: Which sequence, I conceive, is very ill collected.

defs: To assemble together; as, the people collected in a crowd; to accumulate; as, snow collects in banks.

quotes: Whence some collect that the former word imports a plurality of persons.

records.ety: [LL. collecta, fr. L. collecta a collection in money; an assemblage, fr. collerige: cf. F. collecte. See Collect, v. t.]

defs: A short, comprehensive prayer, adapted to a particular day, occasion, or condition, and forming part of a liturgy.
quotes: The noble poem on the massacres of Piedmont is strictly a collect in verse.
